item.title: Attentiveness
item.description: Attentiveness is the first phase of Joan Tronto’s four-phase model of care and the ethical disposition that makes care possible. It names the capacity to notice that care is needed — to perceive the vulnerability or dependency of another without filtering that perception through abstract principle or self-interest. Without attentiveness, the need for care goes unrecognized, and the remaining phases (taking responsibility, performing the care, and assessing responsiveness) never begin.

item.title: Dependency
item.description:
Dependency is the condition of requiring care from others. Care ethics treats dependency not as a deficiency or deviation from an ideal of autonomy but as a fundamental feature of human existence. All persons are dependent at some point — as infants, as ill or aging bodies, as members of communities — and many are dependent throughout their lives in ways that liberal political theory systematically renders invisible.

The fiction of the autonomous, independent rational agent — the subject of social contract theory, of classical economics, of rights-based liberalism — is produced by ignoring the dependency relations that sustain it. The “independent” subject is always already supported by care work that has been made invisible: feeding, cleaning, teaching, nursing, comforting. Every self-sufficient adult was once an infant who would have died without years of intensive care. Every productive worker depends on someone preparing food, maintaining a home, tending children, and caring for the elderly. Dependency is not the exception to autonomy; autonomy is the ideological product of unacknowledged dependency.

item.title: Engrossment
item.description:
Engrossment is Nel Noddings’s term for the receptive mode of consciousness in which the one-caring attends to the cared-for. It is not projection, analysis, or evaluation. It is a turning of attention toward the other in which the carer receives the other’s reality — their feelings, their situation, their expressed needs — on the other’s own terms. Together with motivational displacement, engrossment constitutes the caring relation in Noddings’s framework.

Engrossment is distinguished from empathy in the standard psychological sense. Empathy, as commonly understood, involves imagining what the other feels — putting oneself in their shoes, constructing a model of their inner state from one’s own experiential repertoire. Engrossment is more direct and less mediated. The one-caring does not ask “what would I feel in their position?” but rather lets the other’s experience present itself and attends to it as given. The carer’s own projects, assumptions, and categories are temporarily displaced — not suppressed but backgrounded — so that the other’s reality can come forward.

item.title: Motivational Displacement
item.description:
Motivational displacement is Nel Noddings’s term for the shift in motivation that occurs when a person moves from pursuing their own projects to acting on behalf of the cared-for. Together with engrossment, it constitutes the caring relation. Where engrossment is the receptive phase — attending to the other’s reality — motivational displacement is the active phase: the carer’s energy and attention flow toward what the cared-for needs.

The distinction from altruism matters. Altruism, as typically understood in moral philosophy, is a rational decision to prioritize others’ interests over one’s own — a deliberate, principled choice. Motivational displacement is not a choice derived from principle but a response that arises from engrossment. Having genuinely received the other’s reality, the carer finds themselves moved to act by the other’s situation rather than by duty, rule, or calculated outcome. When care functions well, the movement from attending to acting does not require an intervening deliberation about whether one ought to help. The response flows from the relation itself.

item.title: Particular Other
item.description:
The particular other is the concrete, specific person who stands in a care relation — as opposed to the “generalized other” of Kantian and contractualist ethics. Care ethics insists that moral life does not begin with abstract others who are interchangeable bearers of rights but with specific persons whose needs, histories, and vulnerabilities are irreducibly their own.

Nel Noddings argues that genuine care is always directed at a particular other: this person, in this situation, with these expressed needs. The ethical demand is not “what would a rational agent owe to any person?” but “what does this person, whom I am attending to, need from me now?” The move from the particular to the universal — which Kantian ethics treats as the very definition of moral reasoning — is, for care ethics, a move away from the moral situation rather than toward it. When the parent asks “what does my child need?” they are not applying a universal rule. They are responding to a specific person in a specific situation, and the quality of their response depends on the quality of their attention to that specificity.

item.title: Responsiveness
item.description:
Responsiveness is the fourth and final phase in Joan Tronto’s model of care and the criterion by which the adequacy of care is judged. After care has been noticed (attentiveness), taken up as responsibility (taking care of), and performed as concrete labor (care-giving), responsiveness asks: did the care actually meet the need? How does the cared-for receive and respond to the care given?

Responsiveness rejects the assumption that the carer’s intentions or efforts are sufficient. Good care is not defined by what the carer meant to do but by what the cared-for experienced. A meal prepared with love but that the person cannot eat has failed the responsiveness test. A policy designed to help a population but that the population experiences as invasive has failed. The carer’s sincerity is real but not the measure — the cared-for’s response is.

item.title: Vulnerability
item.description:
Vulnerability is the susceptibility to harm, suffering, or unmet need that care ethics identifies as a universal feature of embodied existence. Unlike liberal political theory, which treats vulnerability as an exceptional condition that befalls otherwise autonomous agents, care ethics holds that vulnerability is constitutive — humans are vulnerable beings from birth to death, and the structures built to deny this produce rather than resolve ethical failure.

Joan Tronto argues that vulnerability is distributed unequally: those with less power, fewer resources, or less social recognition are more exposed to harm and less able to secure the care they need. But the inequality is not just in the distribution of vulnerability — it is in the distribution of attention to vulnerability. Privilege operates through organized inattention: the comfortable do not see the precariousness that sustains their comfort. The political organization of vulnerability — who is protected, who is abandoned, who bears the costs of others’ protection — is a central concern of care ethics as political theory.
